title: Condemning Turkey for its illegal occupation of Cyprus and encouraging President Trump to make the resolution of the Cyprus problem a top foreign policy priority.
plain_summary: This resolution condemns Turkey for occupying part of Cyprus since 1974, which Congress says is illegal under international law. The resolution accuses Turkey of bringing in illegal settlers, destroying religious sites, and refusing to work with international courts. Congress calls on Turkey to remove all its troops from Cyprus and follow NATO's democratic rules. The resolution also encourages President Trump to make solving the Cyprus problem one of his top foreign policy goals.
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Foreign Affairs.
subjects: Sovereignty, recognition, national governance and status

sections.title: Resolution Title
sections.text: This resolution condemns Turkey for its illegal occupation of Cyprus and encourages President Trump to make solving the Cyprus problem a top foreign policy priority.

sections.title: Background Information
sections.text: Turkey has been a member of NATO (North Atlantic Treaty Organization) since nineteen fifty-two, making it a treaty ally of the United States. Modern Turkey was founded on the principles of republicanism and laicite (separation of religion and government). However, Turkish President Recep Tayyip Erdogan, who has led Turkey as an authoritarian leader since two thousand three, has based his decisions on an extremist form of religio-nationalism (mixing religious beliefs with extreme nationalism). Under President Erdogan, Turkey seeks to establish itself as the dominant power in the Eastern Mediterranean, North Africa, and the Caucasus regions. This has led Turkey to take provocative actions against NATO allies, the European Union, and the broader region. President Erdogan's expansionist political ideology requires aggression toward Cyprus.

sections.title: Turkey's Violations in Cyprus
sections.text: The Government of Turkey ordered its military to invade the Republic of Cyprus in nineteen seventy-four. This invasion violated several international treaties including the Treaty of Establishment, the Treaty of Alliance, the North Atlantic Treaty, and the Treaty of Guarantee. These treaties had established the Republic of Cyprus and guaranteed its independence. The invasion also violated the United Nations Charter and customary international law. Since the occupation began, the Government of Turkey has allowed or helped in the destruction of more than five hundred Orthodox Christian churches and religious sites in the territory of Cyprus that the Turkish military occupies. Turkey has also brought in over two hundred thousand illegal settlers into the occupied area of Cyprus to change the social, political, and demographic makeup of the area. This violates the Geneva Conventions (international laws about war and occupation).

sections.title: Court Decisions and Ongoing Violations
sections.text: Turkey is a member state of the European Court of Human Rights. This court has issued decisions and judgments confirming that people, including United States citizens, who hold title to real property in the area of Cyprus occupied by the Turkish military are the only rightful owners of that property. However, Turkey has refused to comply with these court judgments. Despite agreeing to do so, Turkey has failed to support international efforts to find and identify the remains of missing people, including four United States citizens who remain missing since the Turkish invasion of Cyprus in nineteen seventy-four. Turkey also illegally violates the Cyprus Exclusive Economic Zone (the area of ocean where Cyprus has special rights to natural resources). President Erdogan has illegally reopened the beach in Varosha, a once-thriving tourist town from which the Turkish military ethnically cleansed (forcibly removed) its Greek Cypriot population in nineteen seventy-four. President Erdogan has openly called for a two-state solution regarding Cyprus, which violates United Nations resolutions, international law, and the position of the United States Government. The US position is that a Cyprus solution should be within the framework of a bizonal, bicommunal federation (two zones with two communities under one federal government).

sections.title: House Resolutions
sections.text: The House of Representatives strongly condemns Turkey's continued occupation of Cyprus and calls upon Turkey to immediately withdraw all of its estimated thirty-five thousand occupying troops from the island. The House calls upon Turkey to adhere to the democratic principles and mission of the North Atlantic Treaty Organization. The House repeats that the settlement of the Cyprus problem needs to be based on a bizonal, bicommunal federation in a state with single sovereignty and international personality. This entity should embrace constitutional democratic norms and American principles, the European Union acquis Communautaire and EU Founding Treaty (the body of EU laws), United Nations resolutions on Cyprus, and relevant decisions of the European Court of Human Rights and other European courts. The House calls on Turkey to stop its illegal interference in the Cyprus Exclusive Economic Zone. The House calls on the Turkish Government to provide ways for American citizens to seek financial remedies or restitution (money to make up for losses) for their property losses in the Turkish-occupied territory of Cyprus. The House demands Turkey's cooperation in helping recover the four American citizens who are still missing as a result of the nineteen seventy-four invasion of Cyprus. The House calls on Turkey to remove its over two hundred thousand illegal settlers from Turkish-occupied Cyprus and assist in helping Greek-Cypriot refugees return to their ancestral homes. Finally, the House encourages President Trump to make solving the Cyprus problem a top foreign policy priority for his Administration.
